beats.narration_text: So a free packet spreads because its Fourier components ride the quadratic dispersion, energy going as k squared, and the fast momenta outrun the slow ones. I want to make that quantitative. Please: (1) derive why the doubling time scales as sigma-zero squared times mass over h-bar, (2) plug in an electron pinned to 1 nanometer and give me the actual doubling time in femtoseconds, and (3) contrast this with a photon's linear dispersion to show precisely why light in vacuum keeps its shape.

props.segment: Why a Quantum Particle Spreads Out on Its Own
props.command: So a free packet spreads because its Fourier components ride the quadratic dispersion, energy going as k squared, and the fast momenta outrun the slow ones. I want to make that quantitative. Please: (1) derive why the doubling time scales as sigma-zero squared times mass over h-bar, (2) plug in an electron pinned to 1 nanometer and give me the actual doubling time in femtoseconds, and (3) contrast this with a photon's linear dispersion to show precisely why light in vacuum keeps its shape.

beats.narration_text: A baseball thrown in a straight line keeps its shape. A quantum particle released as a tidy packet inevitably smears wider — and the tighter you pin it, the faster it blurs.

card.copy: Why a Quantum Particle Spreads Out on Its Own

props.segment: Why a Quantum Particle Spreads Out on Its Own
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: a narrow wave packet on the left decomposing into a wide spectrum of momentum components, and a wide packet on the right with a narrow momentum spectrum — show the Fourier transform pairs"

beats.narration_text: A localized quantum particle is not a single pure frequency — it is a bundle of many momenta, each with its own wave. The narrow the packet, the wider the spread of momenta inside it.
The packet starts tight. After some time it has smeared across a much wider region. No collision, no interaction — the particle just spread. How does it blur on its own?

card.copy: A tidy quantum packet. No forces. No collisions. It still spreads wider with time. How does a particle blur on its own?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The packet starts tight. After some time it has smeared across a much wider region. No collision, no interaction — the particle just spread. How does it blur on

props.segment: Why a Quantum Particle Spreads Out on Its Own
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: fast and slow Fourier components of a wave packet separating over time — high-k components race ahead, low-k components fall behind, packet stretches"

beats.narration_text: For a free particle, energy scales as k squared — higher momentum, faster speed. The fast components in the packet race ahead; the slow components fall behind.

props.segment: Why a Quantum Particle Spreads Out on Its Own
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: a Gaussian wave packet stretching over time as its Fourier components travel at different speeds — show the packet width growing and the peak shrinking"

beats.narration_text: As time passes, the fast waves pull ahead and the slow waves lag. The packet stretches because its Fourier components all travel at different speeds.

props.segment: Why a Quantum Particle Spreads Out on Its Own
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: compare a wide initial packet (slow spreading) with a tight initial packet (fast spreading) — animate both over the same time interval and show widths growing"

beats.narration_text: The spread grows with time. And the more tightly localized the original packet, the wider its momentum spread, so the faster the fast components run away. Tight start, fast spreading.

props.segment: Why a Quantum Particle Spreads Out on Its Own
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: plot the doubling time formula sigma0^2 * mass / hbar as a function of initial packet width sigma0 — show how tighter initial packets lead to faster doubling"

beats.narration_text: This is the same reason a drum beat sounds sharp up close but echoes far away — each partial travels at its speed and the group loses coherence. For quantum mechanics, the time scale for doubling the width goes as sigma-zero squared times mass over h-bar.

props.segment: Why a Quantum Particle Spreads Out on Its Own
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: compare linear dispersion (photon: no spreading) vs quadratic dispersion (electron: spreading) — plot omega vs k and show why quadratic causes component speeds to differ"

beats.narration_text: This spreading is not disorder or heat — it is purely the quantum dispersion relation. A photon in vacuum doesn't spread this way because its dispersion is linear, not quadratic. An electron's quadratic dispersion is what makes it blur.

props.segment: Why a Quantum Particle Spreads Out on Its Own
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: an electron pinned to 1 nm width doubling in 0.3 femtoseconds, and one pinned to 0.1 nm doubling in 3 attoseconds — show both on a timeline with femtosecond scale"

beats.narration_text: Illustrative example: an electron pinned to 1 nanometer at time zero. Its width doubles in about 0.3 femtoseconds — roughly the time it takes light to cross an atom. Tighten the pin to 0.1 nanometers and the doubling time shrinks to 3 attoseconds.
A localized particle is a bundle of momenta. High momentum moves faster. The bundle spreads. The tighter you started, the faster you spread.

card.copy: Localized = bundle of momenta. High momentum moves faster. The bundle spreads. Tighter start: faster spread.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: A localized particle is a bundle of momenta. High momentum moves faster. The bundle spreads. The tighter you started, the faster you spread.

beats.narration_text: Your wave packet is spreading as we speak. Paste this in and watch the width grow.

props.segment: Why a Quantum Particle Spreads Out on Its Own
props.command: claude "simulate a Gaussian wave packet in free space spreading over time — show the width growing and explain why components at different k travel at different speeds"
